Section heading: Our use of cookies

**Session 2022-23**
chunks.display_text:
Section heading: Our use of cookies

**Session 2022-23**

chunks.text:
Section heading: Online Safety Bill

**Written evidence submitted by the Mental Health Foundation (OSB89)**

**Public Bill Committee, Online Safety Bill**

**About the Mental Health Foundation**

1.Since 1949, the Mental Health Foundation (MHF) has been the UK’s leading charity for everyone’s mental health. With prevention at the heart of what it does, MHF aims to find and address the sources of mental health problems so that people and communities can thrive.

**Summary**

2.NHS Mental Health referrals have reached a record high,\[1\] and recent research puts the UK cost of mental health problems at £118bn every year.\[2\]

3.We cannot simply treat our way out of the huge and rising numbers of mental health problems in the country. The Government has acknowledged that a cross-Government approach is needed to improve the population’s mental health. But it has not yet taken some of the opportunities presented by this Bill to put this in place.

4.We now spend much of our lives online, and the impact of the online environment on our mental health – for good and bad – cannot be overstated.

5.The Online Safety Bill is an ambitious piece of legislation which rightly aims to tame the ‘wild west’ of the internet in a proportionate manner. Many of its measures, particularly those aimed at stopping abuse and bullying, are likely to help support people’s mental health.

6.The Bill does not, though, attempt to tackle some of the other major drivers of poor mental health that are present in the online environment.
chunks.display_text:
Section heading: Online Safety Bill

**Written evidence submitted by the Mental Health Foundation (OSB89)**

**Public Bill Committee, Online Safety Bill**

**About the Mental Health Foundation**

1.Since 1949, the Mental Health Foundation (MHF) has been the UK’s leading charity for everyone’s mental health. With prevention at the heart of what it does, MHF aims to find and address the sources of mental health problems so that people and communities can thrive.

**Summary**

2.NHS Mental Health referrals have reached a record high,\[1\] and recent research puts the UK cost of mental health problems at £118bn every year.\[2\]

3.We cannot simply treat our way out of the huge and rising numbers of mental health problems in the country. The Government has acknowledged that a cross-Government approach is needed to improve the population’s mental health. But it has not yet taken some of the opportunities presented by this Bill to put this in place.

4.We now spend much of our lives online, and the impact of the online environment on our mental health – for good and bad – cannot be overstated.

5.The Online Safety Bill is an ambitious piece of legislation which rightly aims to tame the ‘wild west’ of the internet in a proportionate manner. Many of its measures, particularly those aimed at stopping abuse and bullying, are likely to help support people’s mental health.

6.The Bill does not, though, attempt to tackle some of the other major drivers of poor mental health that are present in the online environment.

chunks.text: internet in a proportionate manner. Many of its measures, particularly those aimed at stopping abuse and bullying, are likely to help support people’s mental health. 6.The Bill does not, though, attempt to tackle some of the other major drivers of poor mental health that are present in the online environment. 7.Two of the major ways in which we know people’s mental health and wellbeing can be harmed are content which **damages their body image**, and content which **promotes or glorifies suicide and self-harm**. 8.Much of this content is delivered algorithmically, yet the Bill does not go far enough in giving users control of this content. It will also not regulate suicide forums, a disturbing and dangerous part of the online environment. 9.This briefing sets out the evidence for why these harms should be addressed, and explains how the Bill could be improved to better protect the public’s mental health. 10.The Bill should: i.Give all users, including children, easily-accessible control of the algorithms which serve them content. This should include advertising content. ii.Cover *the most high-risk* onlineservices, in particular suicide forums, rather than just the *largest* providers. iii.Cover body- and face-editing apps, restricting children’s and younger teenagers’ access to them. i.**Algorithmic recommendation** 11.Algorithmic serving of content is a defining feature of most social media. Much of the time it plays a benign role in providing users with content that they are most likely to be interested in, as indicated through their patterns of online interaction. 12.The damage done by algorithms comes when they function in a way that over-exposes users to particular types of content. In this evidence we focus on content that damages the mental health of those users.
chunks.display_text: 7.Two of the major ways in which we know people’s mental health and wellbeing can be harmed are content which **damages their body image**, and content which **promotes or glorifies suicide and self-harm**. 8.Much of this content is delivered algorithmically, yet the Bill does not go far enough in giving users control of this content. It will also not regulate suicide forums, a disturbing and dangerous part of the online environment. 9.This briefing sets out the evidence for why these harms should be addressed, and explains how the Bill could be improved to better protect the public’s mental health. 10.The Bill should: i.Give all users, including children, easily-accessible control of the algorithms which serve them content. This should include advertising content. ii.Cover *the most high-risk* onlineservices, in particular suicide forums, rather than just the *largest* providers. iii.Cover body- and face-editing apps, restricting children’s and younger teenagers’ access to them. i.**Algorithmic recommendation** 11.Algorithmic serving of content is a defining feature of most social media. Much of the time it plays a benign role in providing users with content that they are most likely to be interested in, as indicated through their patterns of online interaction. 12.The damage done by algorithms comes when they function in a way that over-exposes users to particular types of content. In this evidence we focus on content that damages the mental health of those users.

chunks.text: are most likely to be interested in, as indicated through their patterns of online interaction. 12.The damage done by algorithms comes when they function in a way that over-exposes users to particular types of content. In this evidence we focus on content that damages the mental health of those users. 13.The Bill seeks to address algorithmic serving of harmful content in two main ways: requiring companies to carry out risk assessments and address the way their systems function, and empowering users to choose what material they receive.\[3\] This is welcome, but it does not go as far as it needs to. 14.There are some important measures to protect children from some of this content. It is not clear at present, though, how some categories of harm, including material that damages people’s body image, will be regulated – or if it will be regulated at all. 15.Young people, in particular, can face a torrent of images of their friends, acquaintances, and strangers which are edited digitally, overwhelmingly representing a beauty standard which is unattainable. 16.Our research has shown how harmful material that damages people’s body image can be to young people’s mental health, causing them to feel shame, anxiety, and even experience suicidal thoughts due to their inability to ‘measure up’ to the edited bodies and faces they are constantly confronted with on social media.\[4\]
chunks.display_text: 13.The Bill seeks to address algorithmic serving of harmful content in two main ways: requiring companies to carry out risk assessments and address the way their systems function, and empowering users to choose what material they receive.\[3\] This is welcome, but it does not go as far as it needs to. 14.There are some important measures to protect children from some of this content. It is not clear at present, though, how some categories of harm, including material that damages people’s body image, will be regulated – or if it will be regulated at all. 15.Young people, in particular, can face a torrent of images of their friends, acquaintances, and strangers which are edited digitally, overwhelmingly representing a beauty standard which is unattainable. 16.Our research has shown how harmful material that damages people’s body image can be to young people’s mental health, causing them to feel shame, anxiety, and even experience suicidal thoughts due to their inability to ‘measure up’ to the edited bodies and faces they are constantly confronted with on social media.\[4\]

chunks.text: research has shown how harmful material that damages people’s body image can be to young people’s mental health, causing them to feel shame, anxiety, and even experience suicidal thoughts due to their inability to ‘measure up’ to the edited bodies and faces they are constantly confronted with on social media.\[4\] 17.The Government Equality Office carried out research which found that users were often exposed to content they did not want *‘as a result of the way many social media algorithms work* *(e.g. if one user ‘likes’ an image, this image can appear in the newsfeed of many of their followers).* It went on to say: *‘This is significant given that many participants in our primary research expressed a desire to be more in control of the types of content they engage with on social media.’ \[5\]* 18.Adolescence, in particular, is a critical period, when peer relationships are of increasing significance and importance. Concerns about body image often begin during this time; if these develop into negative feelings about one’s body, they can then last a lifetime.\[6\] 19.Research has found that higher body dissatisfaction is associated with a poorer quality of life and psychological distress,\[7\] a higher likelihood of depression symptoms \[8\],\[9\] and the risk of unhealthy eating behaviours and eating disorders.\[10\],\[11\] 20.The association with eating disorders is particularly worrying: anorexia has the highest mortality rate of any mental health problem. Prior to the pandemic, PwC research estimated that eating disorders cost the state around £15 billion per annum. We know that rates are far higher now, with the NHS struggling to provide specialist treatment to those who need it.\[12\],\[13\]
chunks.display_text: 17.The Government Equality Office carried out research which found that users were often exposed to content they did not want *‘as a result of the way many social media algorithms work* *(e.g. if one user ‘likes’ an image, this image can appear in the newsfeed of many of their followers).* It went on to say: *‘This is significant given that many participants in our primary research expressed a desire to be more in control of the types of content they engage with on social media.’ \[5\]* 18.Adolescence, in particular, is a critical period, when peer relationships are of increasing significance and importance. Concerns about body image often begin during this time; if these develop into negative feelings about one’s body, they can then last a lifetime.\[6\] 19.Research has found that higher body dissatisfaction is associated with a poorer quality of life and psychological distress,\[7\] a higher likelihood of depression symptoms \[8\],\[9\] and the risk of unhealthy eating behaviours and eating disorders.\[10\],\[11\] 20.The association with eating disorders is particularly worrying: anorexia has the highest mortality rate of any mental health problem. Prior to the pandemic, PwC research estimated that eating disorders cost the state around £15 billion per annum. We know that rates are far higher now, with the NHS struggling to provide specialist treatment to those who need it.\[12\],\[13\]

chunks.text: anorexia has the highest mortality rate of any mental health problem. Prior to the pandemic, PwC research estimated that eating disorders cost the state around £15 billion per annum. We know that rates are far higher now, with the NHS struggling to provide specialist treatment to those who need it.\[12\],\[13\] 21.The Bill includes provisions on user empowerment, which will require providers to introduce measures to give adult users control over the content they see. In their current form, however, these provisions introduce few clear requirements on provider companies, and will not apply to child users (that is, to those under the age of 18). This is despite children and young people expressing their concern to us on this issue: 84% of those questioned in a recent survey by The Mix said it was a key issue that the Bill should address.\[14\] 22.We have developed the following recommendations for how the Bill could be strengthened. Taken together, these recommendations would provide users with additional safeguards, where those safeguards are necessary, as well as support the recovery of users who have been struggling with their mental health. **Recommendations**
chunks.display_text: 21.The Bill includes provisions on user empowerment, which will require providers to introduce measures to give adult users control over the content they see. In their current form, however, these provisions introduce few clear requirements on provider companies, and will not apply to child users (that is, to those under the age of 18). This is despite children and young people expressing their concern to us on this issue: 84% of those questioned in a recent survey by The Mix said it was a key issue that the Bill should address.\[14\] 22.We have developed the following recommendations for how the Bill could be strengthened. Taken together, these recommendations would provide users with additional safeguards, where those safeguards are necessary, as well as support the recovery of users who have been struggling with their mental health. **Recommendations**

chunks.text: that the Bill should address.\[14\] 22.We have developed the following recommendations for how the Bill could be strengthened. Taken together, these recommendations would provide users with additional safeguards, where those safeguards are necessary, as well as support the recovery of users who have been struggling with their mental health. **Recommendations** 23.*The user empowerment provisions \[15\] *in the Bill only apply to adults, rather than children. We understand that the Government is of the view that their other measures to protect children online would render giving children control of algorithmic content unnecessary. While the Bill rightly protects children from content that is deemed to be legal but harmful, it does not contain safeguards for content that will be harmful to users who have specific individual mental health vulnerabilities. For example, beauty or fitness-related content could be psychologically dangerous to a child who is recovering from an eating disorder. Therefore, **any empowerment requirement that is extended to adults should apply equally to children and young people under the age of 18.**** * 24.*The House of Lords Digital and Communications Committee recently recommended that* ‘\[…\] the Online Safety Bill should require category 1 platforms to give users a comprehensive toolkit of settings, overseen by Ofcom, allowing users to decide what types of content they see and from whom. Platforms should be required to make these tools easy to find and use. The safest settings should always be the default.’ *\[16\]* ***We agree with these recommendations.*** 25.*Additional safeguards are required for children and young people. We recommend that as well as being easy to find and use, **users should be able to update their algorithmic preferences at any time by flagging content that they find upsetting or do not want to see more of.***
chunks.display_text: 23.*The user empowerment provisions \[15\] *in the Bill only apply to adults, rather than children. We understand that the Government is of the view that their other measures to protect children online would render giving children control of algorithmic content unnecessary. While the Bill rightly protects children from content that is deemed to be legal but harmful, it does not contain safeguards for content that will be harmful to users who have specific individual mental health vulnerabilities. For example, beauty or fitness-related content could be psychologically dangerous to a child who is recovering from an eating disorder. Therefore, **any empowerment requirement that is extended to adults should apply equally to children and young people under the age of 18.**** * 24.*The House of Lords Digital and Communications Committee recently recommended that* ‘\[…\] the Online Safety Bill should require category 1 platforms to give users a comprehensive toolkit of settings, overseen by Ofcom, allowing users to decide what types of content they see and from whom. Platforms should be required to make these tools easy to find and use. The safest settings should always be the default.’ *\[16\]* ***We agree with these recommendations.*** 25.*Additional safeguards are required for children and young people. We recommend that as well as being easy to find and use, **users should be able to update their algorithmic preferences at any time by flagging content that they find upsetting or do not want to see more of.***

chunks.text: these recommendations.*** 25.*Additional safeguards are required for children and young people. We recommend that as well as being easy to find and use, **users should be able to update their algorithmic preferences at any time by flagging content that they find upsetting or do not want to see more of.*** 26.*Providers should be required to* ***push material on social media literacy to users** and **provide the option to receive content that can help with recovery** where it is available, curated by social media companies with the assistance of trusted NGOs or public health bodies.* 27.*Social media companies should be required not only to take into account the harmful nature of content they serve, but also **the amount of time users spend viewing it**, ensuring that harmful material is deprioritised after a specific number of instances are served. How this is done will depend on the nature of the content (for example, longer video content should be treated differently to shorter text-based content), but implementation by providers should be informed by their evidence of how users interact with their service.* 28.*Specific legal but harmful content covered by the Bill will be specified in secondary legislation. **The Government should clarify that this will include material that promotes or glorifies eating disorders and self-harm**. It should also ensure that **material damaging to people’s body** **image** is included. This type of harm functions by accretion: individual images edited to ‘perfect’ people’s bodies may not be harmful, but we know that continued viewing of such images can have major impacts on people’s mental health.\[17\]*
chunks.display_text: 26.*Providers should be required to* ***push material on social media literacy to users** and **provide the option to receive content that can help with recovery** where it is available, curated by social media companies with the assistance of trusted NGOs or public health bodies.* 27.*Social media companies should be required not only to take into account the harmful nature of content they serve, but also **the amount of time users spend viewing it**, ensuring that harmful material is deprioritised after a specific number of instances are served. How this is done will depend on the nature of the content (for example, longer video content should be treated differently to shorter text-based content), but implementation by providers should be informed by their evidence of how users interact with their service.* 28.*Specific legal but harmful content covered by the Bill will be specified in secondary legislation. **The Government should clarify that this will include material that promotes or glorifies eating disorders and self-harm**. It should also ensure that **material damaging to people’s body** **image** is included. This type of harm functions by accretion: individual images edited to ‘perfect’ people’s bodies may not be harmful, but we know that continued viewing of such images can have major impacts on people’s mental health.\[17\]*

chunks.text:
Section heading: Advertising

29.Whilst much content is generated organically by people wanting to present a flattering version of themselves, a proportion is also generated through paid partnerships with fashion and beauty companies.

30.Whilst some of this will involve positive representations of different types of bodies, much of it will not. As with the recommendation above that users should be given explicit control of what content they are algorithmically served, users should also be given control of the categories of advertising they receive.

31.Girlguiding UK has found that adverts on social media are more exploitative as they are targeted based on previous search history and on stereotypical ideas of what young women might be interested in – such as beauty or weight-loss products, as well as being more intrusive, as they appear in places where young people go for social interaction.\[1\]

32.Furthermore, we know from work carried out by the Joint Council for Cosmetic Practitioners (JCCP) \[2\] that illegal advertising of prescription medicines like botulinum toxin (Botox) is commonplace, and not removed under existing systems in a consistent or timely way. The Bill should be amended so that, in addition to requiring providers to take action against fraudulent adverts, it requires them to take action against illegal adverts for beauty treatments.

33.**Recommendation:***users should be given control over the type of advertising they receive in order that they can avoid being presented with excessive advertising showing ‘perfect’ bodies.*

34.**Recommendation:** *the ‘fraudulent advertising’ conditions should be extended to cover illegal advertising of prescription medicines.*
chunks.display_text:
Section heading: Advertising

29.Whilst much content is generated organically by people wanting to present a flattering version of themselves, a proportion is also generated through paid partnerships with fashion and beauty companies.

30.Whilst some of this will involve positive representations of different types of bodies, much of it will not. As with the recommendation above that users should be given explicit control of what content they are algorithmically served, users should also be given control of the categories of advertising they receive.

31.Girlguiding UK has found that adverts on social media are more exploitative as they are targeted based on previous search history and on stereotypical ideas of what young women might be interested in – such as beauty or weight-loss products, as well as being more intrusive, as they appear in places where young people go for social interaction.\[1\]

32.Furthermore, we know from work carried out by the Joint Council for Cosmetic Practitioners (JCCP) \[2\] that illegal advertising of prescription medicines like botulinum toxin (Botox) is commonplace, and not removed under existing systems in a consistent or timely way. The Bill should be amended so that, in addition to requiring providers to take action against fraudulent adverts, it requires them to take action against illegal adverts for beauty treatments.

33.**Recommendation:***users should be given control over the type of advertising they receive in order that they can avoid being presented with excessive advertising showing ‘perfect’ bodies.*

34.**Recommendation:** *the ‘fraudulent advertising’ conditions should be extended to cover illegal advertising of prescription medicines.*

chunks.header: Recognising body image as a harm
chunks.text:
Section heading: Recognising body image as a harm

35.The Government has been clear that only illegal content will be specified as requiring regulation on the face of the Bill, with other types of harmful content that providers should address being set out in secondary regulation.

36.The harm that can result from content that is bad for one’s body image, most notably edited images, is severe. The Government should therefore clarify at this stage that its intention is to include content that damages people’s body image as a specific harm for the regulator to address in the secondary legislation.

37.**Recommendation:**

*The Government should commit to recognising material which damages people’s body image as a serious form of harm in the secondary legislation which will follow the Bill.*

ii.**Regulating risk, not just size of provider**

38.The Bill correctly tries to tread the line between protecting vulnerable people and creating too large a regulatory burden on smaller enterprises.

39.The Bill requires larger social media companies to address content that is ‘legal but harmful’ to adults, but makes no such requirements of smaller providers: at present suicide forums are, therefore, unlikely to be regulated.

40.Suicide forums are commonly used to share information about novel methods of suicide. There is clear evidence that when a particular suicide method becomes better known, the effect is not simply that suicidal people switch from one intended method to the novel one, but that suicides occur in people who would not otherwise have taken their own lives.\[3\]

41.There are therefore important public health reasons to minimise the discussion of dangerous and effective suicide methods.

42.Research has shown that, when visiting dangerous suicide forums, people can find solace in seeing that others have similar experiences of hopelessness.\[4\] This is not an argument for allowing suicide forums to remain active in their current, unregulated form, but for accepting that frank and sometimes disturbing discussions are necessary, given the extent of mental distress that some people experience. The focus of a regulatory approach should be on addressing the promotion and glamourisation of suicide, and detail on methods that can be used.

43.The Bill’s pre-legislative scrutiny committee recommended the legislation ‘adopt a more nuanced approach, based not just on size and high-level functionality, but factors such as risk, reach, user base, safety performance, and business model’.The Government replied that they ‘want the Bill to be targeted and proportionate for businesses and Ofcom and do not wish to impose disproportionate burdens on small companies.’ \[5\]

44.We would suggest that it is entirely appropriate to place a major regulatory burden on small companies who are facilitating the glorification of suicide through their forums, and making it more likely that people will take their own lives.

**Recommendation**

45.*The provisions for regulation of legal but harmful content should be based not only on the size of the provider, but* *also on the risk to people using it, including risk of suicide.*
chunks.display_text:
Section heading: Recognising body image as a harm

35.The Government has been clear that only illegal content will be specified as requiring regulation on the face of the Bill, with other types of harmful content that providers should address being set out in secondary regulation.

36.The harm that can result from content that is bad for one’s body image, most notably edited images, is severe. The Government should therefore clarify at this stage that its intention is to include content that damages people’s body image as a specific harm for the regulator to address in the secondary legislation.

37.**Recommendation:**

*The Government should commit to recognising material which damages people’s body image as a serious form of harm in the secondary legislation which will follow the Bill.*

ii.**Regulating risk, not just size of provider**

38.The Bill correctly tries to tread the line between protecting vulnerable people and creating too large a regulatory burden on smaller enterprises.

39.The Bill requires larger social media companies to address content that is ‘legal but harmful’ to adults, but makes no such requirements of smaller providers: at present suicide forums are, therefore, unlikely to be regulated.

40.Suicide forums are commonly used to share information about novel methods of suicide. There is clear evidence that when a particular suicide method becomes better known, the effect is not simply that suicidal people switch from one intended method to the novel one, but that suicides occur in people who would not otherwise have taken their own lives.\[3\]

41.There are therefore important public health reasons to minimise the discussion of dangerous and effective suicide methods.

42.Research has shown that, when visiting dangerous suicide forums, people can find solace in seeing that others have similar experiences of hopelessness.\[4\] This is not an argument for allowing suicide forums to remain active in their current, unregulated form, but for accepting that frank and sometimes disturbing discussions are necessary, given the extent of mental distress that some people experience. The focus of a regulatory approach should be on addressing the promotion and glamourisation of suicide, and detail on methods that can be used.

43.The Bill’s pre-legislative scrutiny committee recommended the legislation ‘adopt a more nuanced approach, based not just on size and high-level functionality, but factors such as risk, reach, user base, safety performance, and business model’.The Government replied that they ‘want the Bill to be targeted and proportionate for businesses and Ofcom and do not wish to impose disproportionate burdens on small companies.’ \[5\]

44.We would suggest that it is entirely appropriate to place a major regulatory burden on small companies who are facilitating the glorification of suicide through their forums, and making it more likely that people will take their own lives.

**Recommendation**

45.*The provisions for regulation of legal but harmful content should be based not only on the size of the provider, but* *also on the risk to people using it, including risk of suicide.*

chunks.text:
Section heading: Suicide websites

46.Instructional suicide content also exists on static websites with no user involvement. Because the Bill focuses on user-to-user interactions, it will do nothing to address these static webpages which include detailed instructional material on suicide methods.\[6\]

**Recommendation**

47.*The legislation should be extended to cover centrally managed, as well as user-generated, content.*

iii.**Body and face-editing apps**

48.In its original draft form, the Bill was aimed explicitly at regulating user-to-user content and search engines. The Government then added new elements to the Bill, including age-restriction of pornography and tackling scam adverts.

49.We are pleased that the Government has acknowledged that user-to-user communications constitute only one important element of the harms that can be caused by the online environment, as it provides clear scope for regulation of other harmful products, including body and face-editing apps.

50.Our survey of 1,118 teenagers (13-19 years old) revealed that one in four girls and one in ten boys have edited photos of themselves in order to change their face or body shape because of concerns about their body image.\[7\]

51.Image-editing apps are sold using the language of ‘fixing’ or ‘improving’ one’s body, and include functions that specifically allow users to convincingly edit their photos to look thinner, curvier or more muscular, or to otherwise achieve a more conventionally attractive body or face shape.
chunks.display_text:
Section heading: Suicide websites

46.Instructional suicide content also exists on static websites with no user involvement. Because the Bill focuses on user-to-user interactions, it will do nothing to address these static webpages which include detailed instructional material on suicide methods.\[6\]

**Recommendation**

47.*The legislation should be extended to cover centrally managed, as well as user-generated, content.*

iii.**Body and face-editing apps**

48.In its original draft form, the Bill was aimed explicitly at regulating user-to-user content and search engines. The Government then added new elements to the Bill, including age-restriction of pornography and tackling scam adverts.

49.We are pleased that the Government has acknowledged that user-to-user communications constitute only one important element of the harms that can be caused by the online environment, as it provides clear scope for regulation of other harmful products, including body and face-editing apps.

50.Our survey of 1,118 teenagers (13-19 years old) revealed that one in four girls and one in ten boys have edited photos of themselves in order to change their face or body shape because of concerns about their body image.\[7\]

51.Image-editing apps are sold using the language of ‘fixing’ or ‘improving’ one’s body, and include functions that specifically allow users to convincingly edit their photos to look thinner, curvier or more muscular, or to otherwise achieve a more conventionally attractive body or face shape.

chunks.text: of concerns about their body image.\[7\] 51.Image-editing apps are sold using the language of ‘fixing’ or ‘improving’ one’s body, and include functions that specifically allow users to convincingly edit their photos to look thinner, curvier or more muscular, or to otherwise achieve a more conventionally attractive body or face shape. 52.The evidence for the likely damage these apps can cause is set out in our briefing *Image* *\-editing apps and mental health*, which reports on the work of a partnership between the Mental Health Foundation, the University of Birmingham and the Cochrane Common Mental Disorders Group. 53.Whilst the decision to alter images of one’s body and post them online should ultimately be an issue of personal choice for adults, we can protect children from viewing images edited by their peers by introducing age restrictions for their use. 54.Our report for Mental Health Awareness Week 2019 found that one in eight of the adults surveyed experienced suicidal thoughts or feelings because of body image concerns.\[8\] 55.An amendment to the Bill on this topic would be a straightforward way to protect children. Whilst they may still be exposed to edited images from adults, a substantial amount of children and young people’s interaction is with their peers, so such a change would make a real difference to the volume of material they receive. 56.App stores already have guidelines intended to protect children’s mental health. For example, the Google play store states:
chunks.display_text: 52.The evidence for the likely damage these apps can cause is set out in our briefing *Image* *\-editing apps and mental health*, which reports on the work of a partnership between the Mental Health Foundation, the University of Birmingham and the Cochrane Common Mental Disorders Group. 53.Whilst the decision to alter images of one’s body and post them online should ultimately be an issue of personal choice for adults, we can protect children from viewing images edited by their peers by introducing age restrictions for their use. 54.Our report for Mental Health Awareness Week 2019 found that one in eight of the adults surveyed experienced suicidal thoughts or feelings because of body image concerns.\[8\] 55.An amendment to the Bill on this topic would be a straightforward way to protect children. Whilst they may still be exposed to edited images from adults, a substantial amount of children and young people’s interaction is with their peers, so such a change would make a real difference to the volume of material they receive. 56.App stores already have guidelines intended to protect children’s mental health. For example, the Google play store states:

chunks.text: images from adults, a substantial amount of children and young people’s interaction is with their peers, so such a change would make a real difference to the volume of material they receive. 56.App stores already have guidelines intended to protect children’s mental health. For example, the Google play store states: *\[…\] apps that appeal to children but contain adult themes are not allowed, including but not limited to, apps with excessive violence, blood, and gore; apps that depict or encourage harmful and dangerous activities. We also don’t allow apps that promote negative body or* *self image including apps that depict for entertainment purposes, plastic surgery, weight loss, and other cosmetic adjustments to a person's physical appearance.* 57.This guideline could easily be updated to restrict the download of body and face-editing apps to children. Given that two app stores (Apple’s and Google’s) control essentially all of the market in the UK, it would be easily enforceable. The potential mental health benefits of this small change could be very significant. **Recommendation** 58.*The Bill should be amended to mandate age limits for body and face-editing apps. We suggest restricting apps to those aged 16 and above \[9\] *in order to strike a balance between protecting children and not restricting the freedoms of older teenagers. Though these apps present our main area of concern as an organisation, it would make sense to draft any amendment on this topic more broadly in order that it can cover other apps that could be harmful to children, as defined by Ofcom, for example dating apps.** * *June 2022* --- \[1\] Analysis available here. \[2\] See our recent report with the LSE: The economic case for investing in the prevention of mental health conditions in the UK
chunks.display_text: *\[…\] apps that appeal to children but contain adult themes are not allowed, including but not limited to, apps with excessive violence, blood, and gore; apps that depict or encourage harmful and dangerous activities. We also don’t allow apps that promote negative body or* *self image including apps that depict for entertainment purposes, plastic surgery, weight loss, and other cosmetic adjustments to a person's physical appearance.* 57.This guideline could easily be updated to restrict the download of body and face-editing apps to children. Given that two app stores (Apple’s and Google’s) control essentially all of the market in the UK, it would be easily enforceable. The potential mental health benefits of this small change could be very significant. **Recommendation** 58.*The Bill should be amended to mandate age limits for body and face-editing apps. We suggest restricting apps to those aged 16 and above \[9\] *in order to strike a balance between protecting children and not restricting the freedoms of older teenagers. Though these apps present our main area of concern as an organisation, it would make sense to draft any amendment on this topic more broadly in order that it can cover other apps that could be harmful to children, as defined by Ofcom, for example dating apps.** * *June 2022* --- \[1\] Analysis available here. \[2\] See our recent report with the LSE: The economic case for investing in the prevention of mental health conditions in the UK

Freedom of expression in the digital age \[17\] Mental Health Foundation research has shown how harmful material that damages people’s body image can be to young people’s mental health, causing them to feel shame, anxiety, and even experience suicidal thoughts due to their inability to ‘measure up’ to the edited bodies and faces they are constantly confronted with on social media. \[1\] Girlguiding UK letter to the Women and Equalities Select Committee \[2\] Unpublished research – available on request. \[3\] Thomas, K., Chang, SS. & Gunnell, D. Suicide epidemics: the impact of newly emerging methods on overall suicide rates - a time trends study. *BMC Public Health* 11 314 (2011). https://doi.org/10.1186/1471-2458-11-314 \[4\] Biddle, L., Derges, J., Goldsmith, C., Donovan, J. L., & Gunnell, D. (2018). Using the internet for suicide-related purposes: Contrasting findings from young people in the community and self-harm patients admitted to hospital.
